excerpt: Faith is the testimony that there is no god but Allah, alone without partner, and Muhammad is His servant and messenger...obedience to the Imam and acceptance of his words constitute faith itself.

Faith is an act in its entirety (al-īmān ʿamal kulluh). Speech itself is an action—the tongue's action. Belief and the acquisition of knowledge constitute the heart's action. Acting with all bodily organs according to God's command constitutes the body's action.

Wherever Allah mentions faith in the Qur'an, He couples it with righteous deeds. These two matters are indissolubly linked. Although faith and righteous action are conventionally distinguished, their combination alone makes a person a believer.

Faith is conviction of the heart, practice of these convictions with the body, and affirmation of these convictions with the tongue. A person is called a believer because of his affirmation of Allah's promise and threat, and Allah has granted him security from His fatal punishment.
source_author: al-Sharif al-Radi (compiler), attributed to ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib
source_location_hint: Definition of Faith and the believer

excerpt: In Qur'an 2:143, Allah designates prayer itself as faith when He says 'what kind of faith'—alluding to the perfection of prayer as the expression of faith.
source_work: KASHKOLE, Binder 19, Chapter 71 and Ismaili Tafsir traditions
source_location_hint: Faith of the Face and Prayer

excerpt: Death is good news for the believer (al-mawt rīḥānat al-muʾmin). When the soul of my believer reaches this point, it rejoices!
source_author: al-Muʾayyad al-Shīrāzī, citing Imam Jaʿfar al-Ṣādiq
source_location_hint: Pure Faith and the Believer's Death

excerpt: Those to whom the address in the Qur'an is directed—'O you who believe'—are the leaders and princes; all others who bear this address belong to the descendants of ʿAlī ibn Abī Ṭālib. This leadership shall remain in the hands of ʿAlī's descendants as long as those addressed by this title endure.
source_work: KASHKOLE and related Ismaili hadith sources
source_author: The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him)
source_authority: Prophetic tradition as transmitted by Ismaili scholars

excerpt: Believers of lower rank who followed the summons of those of higher rank are thus joined with their spiritual elders and elevated to their station. 'Offspring' here means spiritual or causal descendants, not biological lineage.

Whoever blends his desires with the Messenger's commands and employs opinion, speculation, and independent reasoning to satisfy his appetites is no believer but an innovator. His good deeds, no matter how numerous, will bring him no benefit.
If all the faith were one and there was no deficiency or excess in it, then no believer would have gained superiority over another...those whose faith is flawed will enter Hell.
source_work: Kitāb al-ʿAlim wa-l-Ghulam (or similar Jaʿfar ibn Manṣūr al-Yamān)
source_location_hint: doctrine of faith-degrees in early Ismaili tradition

excerpt: The first believers of the Umma possess superiority over those who believed later...Amir al-Mu'minin Ali was the foremost of all the Companions to embrace faith.

In these verses, the differences between the degrees of faith and the different conditions of the believers have been mentioned...the believers' seeking forgiveness demonstrates the grace of the ancients.
These verses establish the differences in degrees of faith...mere sobriety without faith profits nothing.
source_location_hint: knowledge and faith relationship in Ismaili cosmology

excerpt: Faith rests upon the triple foundation of belief, word, and deed. Without all three, faith cannot exist.
source_location_hint: conditions of faith in Islamic law

excerpt: Inner faith consists in affirming the five principles one has confessed with the tongue. The greater the sincerity, the higher the degrees of inner faith.
Whoever utters La ilaha illa Allah has entered Paradise...Whoever utters La ilaha illa Allah with sincerity has entered Paradise.
The Messenger commanded Say La ilaha illa Allah and you shall prosper. He did not require detailed knowledge but asked only for confession and belief.
source_location_hint: faith-knowledge relationship in Persian Ismaili tradition

excerpt: Faith cannot exist without confirmation, and confirmation cannot be achieved without epistemology. The degrees of faith are erected upon the ladder-rungs of knowledge.
source_work: Rasa'il Ahmadiyyah (Epistles attributed to Imam Ahmad)

excerpt: Islam pertains to outward words and deeds; faith concerns the inner realm—the purity and sincerity of the heart.
When God grants a servant the capacity to recognize Him, His Messenger, and His Imam, and that servant confesses with his tongue, he becomes a believer.
source_location_hint: conditions of inner faith and imamology

excerpt: In La ilaha illa Huwa, the numerical value of the letters equals the numerical value of the name Ali—pointing to the inward invitation.
source_work: Tuhfat al-Sarayir fi Maani Huruf al-Nabi (or similar esoteric commentary)
source_location_hint: abjad numerology and esoteric hermeneutics in Ismaili taʾwīl

excerpt: The numerical sum of the letters of Muhammad's name equals the numerical value of Islam; the numerical sum of Ali's name equals the numerical value of faith
source_location_hint: on sacred nomenclature and numerical correspondence in Ismaili cosmology
